title: Singular Hodge theory of matroids
speaker: Jacob Matherne
abstract: Kazhdan–Lusztig (KL) polynomials for Coxeter groups were introduced in the 1970s, providing deep relationships among representation theory, geometry, and combinatorics. In 2016, Elias, Proudfoot, and Wakefield defined analogous polynomials in the setting of matroids. In this talk, I will compare and contrast KL theory for Coxeter groups with KL theory for matroids.

proof_stages.label: Coxeter Kazhdan-Lusztig template
proof_stages.summary: The talk first recalls the Coxeter-group story: Bruhat order and rank, recursively defined Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials, their Schubert-variety intersection-cohomology interpretation in Weyl type, and positivity for arbitrary Coxeter groups.

proof_stages.label: Lattice of flats and top-heavy inequality
proof_stages.summary: For a central essential hyperplane arrangement, flats are organized by reverse inclusion into a ranked lattice, and the top-heavy theorem for arrangements is stated as the Dowling-Wilson conjecture for arbitrary matroids.

proof_stages.label: Matroid Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials
proof_stages.summary: The characteristic polynomial is introduced as the matroidal analogue of the R-polynomial, localization and contraction supply the recursive intervals, and the Elias-Proudfoot-Wakefield definition of matroid KL polynomials leads to positivity and real-rootedness conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Schubert variety of an arrangement
proof_stages.summary: For a realizable matroid, the Schubert variety is defined as the closure of the arrangement complement embedding into a product of projective lines, and its affine stratification by flats yields ordinary cohomology dimensions and the Björner-Ekedahl injection into intersection cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Geometric proof in the realizable case
proof_stages.summary: Using the affine-cell cohomology count, the Björner-Ekedahl injection, and Hard Lefschetz for intersection cohomology gives the top-heavy inequality, while the local intersection-cohomology Poincaré polynomial gives nonnegative KL coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Matroidal statements without geometry
proof_stages.summary: The same two conjectures make sense for arbitrary matroids because the lattice of flats and characteristic polynomial are combinatorial, but the Schubert variety and its intersection cohomology have no direct geometric meaning.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial replacements for cohomology and resolutions
proof_stages.summary: The ordinary cohomology of the arrangement Schubert variety is replaced by the graded Möbius algebra of the lattice of flats, and the cohomology of an iterated blowup resolution has a parallel generators-and-relations presentation.

proof_stages.label: Singular Hodge-theoretic program for matroids
proof_stages.summary: The proposed program is to decompose the resolution ring as a module over the Möbius algebra, isolate a combinatorial intersection-cohomology summand, and prove injection, Hard Lefschetz, Poincaré duality/Hodge-Riemann properties sufficient to imply top-heaviness and KL positivity for all matroids.

examples.name: Three central lines in a plane
examples.note: The arrangement of three lines through the origin makes the lattice of flats, characteristic-polynomial point count, trivial KL polynomial, and local singular point of the Schubert variety concrete, but no argument depends on this specific arrangement.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws the three-line arrangement, builds its lattice of flats rank by rank, and computes its characteristic polynomial by inclusion-exclusion.

examples.name: Coxeter arrangement comparison
examples.note: An audience question about the reflection arrangement of a Coxeter group prompts the clarification that Coxeter KL polynomials and matroid KL polynomials are not specializations of one another, though both fit Stanley's more general KL-Stanley framework.
examples.engagement_reason: The Coxeter arrangement is only discussed conceptually as another hyperplane arrangement whose polynomials can be compared via the Kazhdan–Lusztig–Stanley framework, without working out an instance.
